request.question: Girl Alone was sponsored by an American multinational food manufacturing company that produces cereal and convenience foods, and is headquartered where?

title: La Loma Foods
Tee Yih Jia
Ken's Foods
Kellogg's
GoodMark Foods
PepsiCo
Convenience food
Bright Food
Patties Foods
Girl Alone
sentences: La Loma Foods, formerly named Loma Linda Food Company and Loma Linda Foods, and with products presently branded under the name Loma Linda and Loma, is a former food manufacturing company that produced vegetarian and vegan foods.
It is presently an active brand of vegetarian and vegan food products produced and purveyed by the Atlantic Natural Foods Company of Nashville, North Carolina.
Loma Linda Foods began operations in 1905 under the name The Sanitarium Food Company and was owned by the Seventh-day Adventist Church until 1990.
Tee Yih Jia Food Manufacturing Pte Ltd (simplified Chinese: 第一家), is a food manufacturing company headquartered in Singapore.

Tee Yih Jia is the world leading manufacturer of spring roll pastry (also known as "popiah" in local context) that also manufactures a wide range of ready-to-eat Asian convenience foods such as roti paratha, crepes, cocktail prawn rolls, glutinous rice balls, spring rolls and samosas.

Apart from pastry-based food, Tee Yih Jia manufactures other frozen convenience food products such as Dim Sum, Ramen, Oriental Buns (or mantou), and Asian Pizzas with flavours like Peking duck and satay chicken.
Tee Yih Jia's "Spring Home" brand products are manufactured in Singapore as well as in several regional facilities.
Tee Yih Jia has a 400000 sqft factory in Senoko (Singapore) and a Johor (Malaysia) factory of 200000 sqft , as well as manufacturing plants in the USA and China.
Ken's Foods is a privately held food manufacturing company located in Marlborough, Massachusetts.
Ken's primary products are salad dressings, sauces, and marinades.
The company's commercial food manufacturing divisions produce products both for retail sale and food service, including contract manufacturing for companies such as Newman's Own.
The Kellogg Company (also Kellogg's, Kellogg, and Kellogg's of Battle Creek) is an American multinational food manufacturing company headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States.
Kellogg's produces cereal and convenience foods, including cookies, crackers, toaster pastries, cereal bars, fruit-flavored snacks, frozen waffles, and vegetarian foods.

The company's brands include Froot Loops, Apple Jacks, Corn Flakes, Frosted Flakes, Rice Krispies, Special K, Cocoa Krispies, Keebler, Pringles, Pop-Tarts, Kashi, Cheez-It, Eggo, Nutri-Grain, Morningstar Farms, and many more.
Kellogg's stated purpose is "Nourishing families so they can flourish and thrive."
Goodmark Foods, Inc. is an American food manufacturing company, based in Raleigh, North Carolina.
It produces "meat snacks, packaged meats, and extruded grain snacks," especially Slim Jim jerky meat snacks.
It is owned by ConAgra.
PepsiCo, Inc. is an American multinational food, snack, and beverage corporation headquartered in Purchase, New York.
PepsiCo has interests in the manufacturing, marketing, and distribution of grain-based snack foods, beverages, and other products.
PepsiCo was formed in 1965 with the merger of the Pepsi-Cola Company and Frito-Lay, Inc.

PepsiCo has since expanded from its namesake product Pepsi to a broader range of food and beverage brands, the largest of which included an acquisition of Tropicana Products in 1998 and the Quaker Oats Company in 2001, which added the Gatorade brand to its portfolio.
Convenience food, or tertiary processed food, is food that is commercially prepared (often through processing) to optimise ease of consumption.
Such food is usually ready to eat without further preparation.
It may also be easily portable, have a long shelf life, or offer a combination of such convenient traits.
Although restaurant meals meet this definition, the term is seldom applied to them.
Convenience foods include ready-to-eat dry products, frozen foods such as TV dinners, shelf-stable foods, prepared mixes such as cake mix, and snack foods.
Bright Food (officially Bright Food (Group) Co., Ltd.) is a multinational food and beverages manufacturing company headquartered in Shanghai, China.
It is the second-largest China-based food manufacturing company measured by 2011 revenues.
The company is wholly owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government via Shanghai Municipal Investment Group and another sister company.
Patties Foods, is an Australian food manufacturing company that produces meat pies, baked goods, frozen fruits, and pre-made desserts.
Patties Foods is represented in the Australian market by the Four'N Twenty, Patties, Herbert Adams, Creative Gourmet, Nanna's, Chefs Pride and Snowy River brands.
Patties is the largest Meat pie producing company in Australia, and the world.
They are the producers of several well-known Meat pie's, including the Patties, Snowy River pie, Herbert Adams pie, and as of 2003, Four'N Twenty pies.
Patties Foods is headquartered in Bairnsdale, Victoria.
Girl Alone was an American radio soap opera broadcast on NBC from 1935 to 1941.
Sponsored by Kellogg's and Quaker Oats, the series was scripted by Fayette Krum.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are the first-hop summarizer. Compress the supplied passages into a short, actionable intermediate state that lets the next agent know exactly what is present in the passages, what is missing, and what to fetch next. Produce a single `summary` string only.

Summary content requirements (concise: a few short sentences or clauses):
- First-hop evidence: one short paraphrase or a very brief quoted phrase from the passages that links the relevant entities or facts supporting any partial answer. Do not copy long verbatim spans.
- Bridge entity: explicitly name the person/entity mentioned in the passages that the second hop should investigate (use the full name or label exactly as it appears in the passages). If the question uses only a given name and the passages do not disambiguate which individual is meant, state clearly that the identity is missing/ambiguous.
- Missing fact: state in one short sentence which specific fact is required to answer the question (for example: winner name, year, birthplace, or another identifying detail) and confirm it is not present in the given passages.
- Retrieval directions: give one concise, ordered, actionable next-hop plan (1–2 steps). Prefer concrete target pages or query patterns, e.g. "open the named person's biography to check for [missing fact]" or a short query pattern such as '"<NAME>" birthplace OR "born in" site:wikipedia.org' or 'open a chronological list of <AWARD> winners and search for <NAME>'. Do not instruct the system to execute retrieval here — only specify what the next retrieval should fetch.
- Clarification: if ambiguity persists (e.g., only a given name is provided and multiple candidates are possible), include a short clarifying request asking the user to provide the full name.

Hard constraints and tone:
- Be concise and factual. Keep the same neutral, summary tone as before.
- Do not guess, infer, or assert any facts not present in the passages (no candidate answers, no years, no birthplace assertions, etc.).
- Do not ask the user for permission to search external sources. Give explicit next-hop retrieval directions instead.
- Output only the single `summary` field text complying with the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict: Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

You are integrating newly retrieved passages (second‑hop evidence) with the first‑hop context to produce a grounded, post‑retrieval summary that preserves the facts the final answer generator will need. Produce a single field `summary` whose value is 1–3 concise sentences that: (1) directly state the answer to the question (e.g., where the studios/recordings were located) based on the available evidence; (2) if any passage explicitly names a production facility or studio and ties that facility to a specific locality (for example by giving the facility name together with a street, town, or county), include one short supporting sentence that paraphrases that passage to show the link between the facility and the locality; and (3) include one very short sentence or clause that synthesizes the context and the supporting passage to place the studios in the stated locality. Paraphrase supporting passages; do not include long verbatim quotations.

If the passages do not conclusively link a named studio to a specific locality, preserve and state that uncertainty rather than asserting unsupported specifics. Avoid headers, meta commentary, long quotations, extra sections, or inventing details not present in the provided context/passages. Keep phrasing compact, factual, and focused on the evidence needed to support the final answer.

Output contract: produce only the `summary` field (1–3 concise sentences) that integrates the first‑hop context with the second‑hop passages as described above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer` as follows. Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) as the evidential basis for the final answer; do not add external facts except where the instructions below explicitly allow a limited, well‑justified use of internal background knowledge. Always produce the final result inside the [[ ## answer ## ]] block and make that block contain only the minimal answer string required by these rules.

1) General minimal-answer rule
- If the summaries unambiguously support a single short answer (a short name, title, demonym, year, or single noun/phrase of roughly 1–5 words), emit only that minimal token sequence as the answer. Normalize the chosen substring by removing any leading/trailing whitespace or surrounding punctuation but preserve the phrase's internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries. Do not add quotes, em dashes, braces, labels, trailing punctuation, explanatory clauses, or any additional text.

2) When both summaries agree on an identical short phrase
- If summary_1 and summary_2 contain the same short named-entity string (after lowercasing only for agreement detection), extract that common phrase, strip surrounding punctuation/whitespace, and emit it verbatim (preserving original capitalization) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]] with no surrounding or appended characters.

3) Patterned question triggers (minimal formatting + deterministic selection)
- 'Which ... is from X, A or B?': When the question asks which of two named options is from a place and the summaries clearly indicate a single band/person as the answer, emit only the minimal band/person name supported by the summaries.
- Comparative membership questions (e.g., 'Does A or B have more band members?'): If the summaries clearly indicate which band has more members, emit only that band's name.
- Short common-name questions (e.g., 'What do people in the UK commonly call the location ...?'): If both summaries provide the same short common name, emit only that short name string.

4) Nationality questions
- If the question asks for a nationality/demonym and either summary_1 or summary_2 contains a demonym, emit only the minimal demonym token/phrase. Prefer the first demonym found in summary_1; if none appears in summary_1, use the first found in summary_2. Emit it verbatim (no added words or punctuation).

5) Two-performer composition specificity
- If the question asks for the "type of musical composition" used by a named act and both summaries use a generic word like 'song(s)', you may consult internal knowledge only to determine whether the act is a two-person (duo) group. If you can reliably confirm the act is a two-person group, replace the generic term with the single-word or very short noun that names a composition intended for two performers (for example, 'duet') and emit that single word as the minimal answer. If you cannot reliably confirm duo membership, emit the minimal noun exactly as it appears in the summaries.

6) Narrow assumed referent (single-name trigger)
- If the question supplies only a given name (e.g., 'Maurice') and the visible summaries explicitly mention a specific example with that given name (e.g., a named example appears in the summaries) and the summaries identify the requested fact (e.g., the award), then you may assume that referent and emit the minimal answer (for example, a single year) if you have sufficient internal confidence. If you do not have sufficient internal confidence, follow the normal clarification behavior (emit a brief clarifying question as the answer field) rather than guessing.

7) What to avoid (formatting and content prohibitions)
- Never add explanatory text, full sentences, comparisons, justifications, or provenance in the answer field. Do not append decorative punctuation (em dashes, extra commas, trailing periods), quotes, braces, JSON-like structures, leading newlines, or any characters not part of the normalized minimal phrase. Do not invent facts not present in the summaries except where the two narrowly scoped rules above permit limited internal confirmation (duo-confirmation and the single-name assumed referent). If the summaries do not unambiguously support a single minimal answer and you lack permitted internal justification, emit a concise clarification request as the answer.

Additional specific, evidence-directed selection rules (apply these only when their trigger conditions are met, otherwise follow the rules above):

8) Prefer an explicitly named immediate jurisdiction when resolving county/country wording conflicts
- Trigger: The question asks which country/sovereign-state a county or borough is part of (phrasing like 'county' and 'part of what country' or equivalent) and the summaries disagree or one summary uses a parenthetical membership note such as 'A (part of B)'.
- Behavior: If one summary contains a parenthetical of the form 'A (part of B)', treat the pre-parenthetical name A as the immediate administrative region referenced by the summaries and emit A (trimmed of surrounding punctuation/whitespace) as the minimal answer. More generally, when summaries disagree about a place, prefer the summary that directly names the immediate jurisdiction associated with the county (the subnational or constituent area named in the summary) and extract that short named phrase verbatim as the answer. Do not add any extra words or punctuation.
- Fallback: If neither summary clearly names an immediate jurisdiction or both are equally ambiguous, emit a concise clarification question per the base rules rather than guessing.

9) Size-answer simplification when both summaries agree with an inclusive qualifier
- Trigger: The question explicitly asks for a size (phrasing like 'what size' or 'equal to or smaller than what size' or equivalent) AND summary_1 and summary_2 are identical strings for agreement-detection (case-insensitive) AND that common string matches the pattern '<size> or smaller' or '<size> or less' (case-insensitive, allowing optional surrounding punctuation/whitespace).
- Behavior: Produce as the minimal answer only the '<size>' portion of the agreed common phrase by removing the trailing inclusive qualifier 'or smaller' or 'or less' and any immediately adjacent punctuation/extra whitespace. Emit the remaining size token (number plus unit) verbatim as shown in the summaries (preserve digits, unit spelling/capitalization, and internal spacing).
- Do not apply this edit when the summaries do not exactly agree, when the common phrase does not match the pattern, or when the question is not asking for a size.

10) Prefer identical parenthetical short-names when present in both summaries
- Trigger: Both summary_1 and summary_2 each contain an identical parenthetical short-name substring (a substring of the form '(' ... ')' that, once trimmed, is 1–5 words long, agreement checked case-insensitively).
- Behavior: Extract the parenthetical content (remove the surrounding '(' and ')', trim surrounding whitespace and surrounding punctuation) and treat that extracted short-name as the canonical minimal answer. Emit it verbatim (preserving internal characters and capitalization exactly as shown in the summaries) as the sole content of [[ ## answer ## ]]. Do not emit the longer formal name when such an identical parenthetical is present in both summaries.

Output contract reminder: The produced `answer` must be exactly the minimal answer string determined by the rules above (or a short clarification question when required). The [[ ## answer ## ]] block must contain only that string and nothing else.

response.hop1_query: Girl Alone was sponsored by an American multinational food manufacturing company that produces cereal and convenience foods, and is headquartered where?
hop1_docs: Kellogg's | The Kellogg Company (also Kellogg's, Kellogg, and Kellogg's of Battle Creek) is an American multinational food manufacturing company headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States.  Kellogg's produces cereal and convenience foods, including cookies, crackers, toaster pastries, cereal bars, fruit-flavored snacks, frozen waffles, and vegetarian foods.  The company's brands include Froot Loops, Apple Jacks, Corn Flakes, Frosted Flakes, Rice Krispies, Special K, Cocoa Krispies, Keebler, Pringles, Pop-Tarts, Kashi, Cheez-It, Eggo, Nutri-Grain, Morningstar Farms, and many more.  Kellogg's stated purpose is "Nourishing families so they can flourish and thrive."

Bright Food | Bright Food (officially Bright Food (Group) Co., Ltd.) is a multinational food and beverages manufacturing company headquartered in Shanghai, China.  It is the second-largest China-based food manufacturing company measured by 2011 revenues.  The company is wholly owned by the Shanghai Municipal Government via Shanghai Municipal Investment Group and another sister company.

Flavour and Life | Flavour and Life (FAL) Food and Beverages is a multinational food and beverage company headquartered in Sydney, Australia, with interests in the manufacturing, producing, marketing, and distribution of health-based foods, beverages, cosmetics, and other products.  It is best known for its Coco Joy line of coconut-based foods and beverages.
Siniora (company) | Siniora Food and Manufacturing plc.  Is a multinational food industry company that produces meat products in Palestine, Jordan, and Saudi Arabia.

Convenient Food Mart | Convenient Food Mart (CFM) is a chain of convenience stores in the United States.  The private company's headquarters are located in Mentor, Ohio, and there are currently approximately 325 stores located in the US.  Convenient Food Mart operates on the franchise system.

Bell Food Group | The Bell Food Group is the leading meat processor and manufacturer of convenience products in Switzerland.  The company was founded in 1869 by Samuel Bell in Basel, where its headquarters is located up to now.  Its range of products includes meat, poultry, charcuterie, seafood and convenience products.  Brands include Bell, Abraham, ZIMBO, Môssieur Polette and Hilcona.  Some 8,000 employees generated consolidated sales of CHF 2.6 billion in the fiscal year 2014.  Bell is listed on the Swiss stock exchange.  The majority shareholder of Bell is Coop, one of Switzerland's largest retail and wholesale companies.

McKee Foods | McKee Foods Corporation is a privately held and family owned American snack food and granola manufacturer headquartered in Collegedale, Tennessee.  The corporation is the maker of the Little Debbie Snacks, Sunbelt Bakery granola and cereal, Heartland Brands, and Drake's Cakes.  McKee Foods has its own fleet of trucks to distribute its products.
response.summary_1: First-hop evidence: Passage 1 states Kellogg's is "an American multinational food manufacturing company headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States" and produces cereal and convenience foods. Bridge entity: Kellogg's | The Kellogg Company. Missing fact: confirmation that Kellogg's was the sponsor of Girl Alone (the supplied passages do not state the show's sponsor). Retrieval directions: 1) Open the "Girl Alone" article or search '"Girl Alone" sponsor' (e.g., site:wikipedia.org OR "Girl Alone" sponsor Kellogg) to confirm the sponsor; 2) if the sponsor is Kellogg's, open The Kellogg Company (Kellogg's) Wikipedia/official page to verify the headquarters is "Battle Creek, Michigan, United States." Clarification: if multiple companies match the sponsor description, provide the full sponsor name.
response.hop2_query:
1) "Girl Alone sponsor" OR "Girl Alone radio sponsor" OR "Girl Alone (radio) sponsor" site:wikipedia.org
2) "Girl Alone" sponsor Kellogg
3) "Girl Alone" radio soap sponsor
4) "The Kellogg Company" Wikipedia
5) "Kellogg's headquarters" OR "Kellogg Company headquarters" site:wikipedia.org
hop2_docs: Girl Alone | Girl Alone was an American radio soap opera broadcast on NBC from 1935 to 1941.  Sponsored by Kellogg's and Quaker Oats, the series was scripted by Fayette Krum.

Pep (cereal) | Pep was a brand of whole-wheat breakfast cereal produced by the Kellogg Company, and introduced in 1923.  Pep was a long-running rival to Wheaties, and also the sponsor of Mutual Radio's "The Adventures of Superman" radio series.  One of Pep's advertising slogans was "the Sunshine cereal".

The Girls Won't Leave the Boys Alone | The Girls Won't Leave the Boys Alone is an album by Cherish the Ladies released in 2001 on the Windham Hill label.  The title reverses the lyrics "the boys won't leave the girls alone" from the Irish song "Belle of Belfast City/I'll Tell Me Ma", popularized in the album "Irish Heartbeat" by Van Morrison and The Chieftains. " The Boys Won't Leave the Girls Alone" is also the title of a 1962 album by The Clancy Brothers and Tommy Makem.
Alone (The Morning After Girls album) | Alone.  is the second studio album by Australian neo-psychedelia band The Morning After Girls.
You're Never Alone With a Cigarette (Sun City Girls Singles Volume 1) | You're Never Alone With a Cigarette (Sun City Girls Singles Volume 1)

Boys and Girls Alone | Boys and Girls Alone was a British reality TV programme made for Channel 4 and first broadcast in 2009.  Dubbed "Kid Brother" by the press, for its similarity to the channel's major show "Big Brother".

Alone in North America | alone in north america is a 2014 documentary film about the North American tour of the neo-psychedelia band the morning after girls, in support of their LP "alone".  It was produced by David Hilbert, and directed by Hilbert and the morning after girls.  The film showcases music, images and live performances while recounting a novel experience in travel, as band members narrate collective aspirations and motives.
hop2_titles: The Girls Won't Leave the Boys Alone
You're Never Alone With a Cigarette (Sun City Girls Singles Volume 1)
response.summary_2: The show's sponsor, The Kellogg Company (Kellogg's), is headquartered in Battle Creek, Michigan, United States. Passage [1] lists Kellogg's as a sponsor of Girl Alone, linking the program's sponsor to that Battle Creek headquarters.
